sentences.text: I was born in Eastern Poland in 1928, October 10.
Next city calls Zdolbunow, which-- which is a residential town about 10 miles from the largest city called Rovno.
Eastern Europe-- Eastern Poland became a country after 1920, no?
My father was one of five brothers and two sisters.
My mother's one of six brothers and two sisters.
My grandfather was a grain merchant, grain and cattle merchant.
And the-- and the family lived in the town for hundreds of years.
When my dad was drafted in the army, in the Polish, on 1921, after Poland became independent of Russia.
And he served in the army in Krakow in Marshal Pilsudski's elite cavalry unit.
After my dad finished the service, he came back to Zdolbunow.
He was going to visit his uncle in a town called [POLISH], 20 miles from Zdolbunow.
He met my mother on a train, and they got married a few months later.
Then they moved back to Zdolbunow and started their new life.
My dad was dealing in commodities, like grains and hops, and my mother operated a country store.
Hops.
Hops that you make beer from.
You brew beer.
H-O-P-S, hops.
My father's brothers, the four, also were in the same line of business, grain and-- and cattle merchants.
My father was the second oldest of seven children.
Two sisters-- one name was Rachel, and one name is Testle, were married, and their husbands were basically farmers.
His sisters.
My mother's three sisters left in the turn of the century to America.
And the two remaining sisters lived in [POLISH].
[POLISH] was a town surrounded by forest, and the basic industry was lumber and floor coverings and-- and plywood.
About 4,000 Jews lived in [POLISH].
My aunt--
No, it was about 20,000 people.
My aunt was a widow.
Her husband died young, and she had two children.
And she operate a-- a restaurant and a guest house.
My grandmother on my-- and grandfather on my-- on my mother's side-- my grandfather was managing an estate for a prince who basically was farming and lumber.
And my grandmother with her three sons were running a guest house and a-- what do you call?-- a tobacco store.
My other two uncles became lumber engineers-- you know, forest engineers.
And they moved on to a larger city and continued to be forest engineers.

When I reached the age of school age, I-- I went to a school, a private school called Tarbut, which all subjects were taught in Hebrew, but we were taking Polish, Ukrainian, Russian, and other foreign language.
Our town, Zdolbunow, was about a little over 12,000 people, and close to 1,200 Jewish people.
We had a Polish high school, a Ukraine high school, and a Czech high school.
The population of the town comprised of majority Ukrainians, Poles, about 10% of the Jews, and Czechs, and some Germans who were left over from the First World War.
We had a Catholic church, and a-- and a-- and also a-- a Czech church.
We had a Greek Catholic and a Roman Catholic church.
We had a Polish high school.
We had a Czech high school.
We had a private school, Tarbut, and some religious schools.
The neighbors, they were quite friendly.
We-- all the ethnic groups had no ethnical problems.
They were trying to build a new society because leaving the czar, they had to start over from scratch.
And the Polish government awarded some Polish officers some land.
They became land owners and large farmers.
Most of the Jewish people were some-- were professionals and business people.
They operated the breweries and the mills to grind grain for flour.
And they exported, I believe, 70% of the flour from our area was exported to the rest of Poland.
They also exported, as I said before, hops and lumber and agriculture.
Also in our town was a cement factory because we had lime mines.
The most important industry in our city was the railroad.
We had a beautiful railroad station.
It was an important stop because the railroad station repaired all the-- all the machinery for the railroad because it was connecting east and west.
Other words, coming from Russia going to West Europe, you had to pass the station.
The majority of people who worked in the station were the Poles.
The Ukrainians were basically farmers-- small farmers and some larger farmers.
Most of the Czechs had large farms and also were farming the hops for beer.
Between Rovno and Zdolbunow was a big brewery in this town named [POLISH].
Was a Czech village.
While I was attending school, I used to come in the afternoon and work in my mother's store.
I enjoy ?] [? staying ?] at register, make deposit at the bank.
And they used to quiz me in arithmetic.
My-- my mother's store was a country store, where they sold from groceries to whatever they needed, even materials, textiles, and [? all. ?] And life was quite smooth.
We played soccer.
We played volleyball.
We used to skate a lot in the winter, ski a lot and sled.
We played with our non-Jewish friends, the Christians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] We used to attend the holidays.
They would attend our holidays.
We would invite them for Passover Seder, and they were invited-- and they invited us to the-- for Christmas dinner.
What was that?
Because [? lots ?] [? of them ?] was they're killed.
[CRYING]
I remember all the schoolboys from my school.
Only about three of us survived.
Now, [CRYING] the activities, we used to-- well, came back from school, we were allowed to practice, you know, athletics.
And then we took a moment, do our homework.
For some reason, we were trained to read a lot.
And we-- I personally enjoyed reading a lot.
So I stayed up late and read.
And we attended the movie theaters, attended soccer games and all sports in our spare time.
We were too young to get into political discussions, but life was quite smooth.
And the-- as you know, as I told you, Jewish population were basically mostly businessmen, professional.
You had some tailors and cobblers and shoemakers.
But the Ukrainians were farmers.
The Czechs were farmers.
The Poles, some were farm.
The majority worked at the government because the government, the mayor, and the police force were all basically Polish people.
And-- and the--
My family, in Europe, we were [? very-- ?] in our particular town, we didn't have a yeshiva, but we did have two Orthodox shuls.
So basically, every Jew in our area, I would say 99%, were traditional religious-- observed all the holidays and on Saturdays and Fridays attended the synagogue.
The non-Jewish population was also religious and attended their churches on Sunday.
Religion was very well respected by-- by all the total population.
We had, occasionally, guy that didn't believe in anything, but that was a small minority.
That life went on like this until 1937, when the Hitler machine start expanding.
And especially when they occupied Austria and Czechoslovakia, I could detect a nervousness in the community.
More people were reading more newspapers, listening to the radio.
We have picked up refugees that left Austria, Vienna, and Prague.
My dad and some of his friends, since we had a big house, the affluent Jewish community would go to the station and pick up those refugees and bring them to our home to stay with us.
The refugees, majority were physicians and engineers that escaped-- were able to escape.
And that life went on till 1939.
In September 1939, Germany attacked Poland.
We see a bunch of more relatives.
A lot were-- my mother's couple of-- two ladies, very good friends, came in.
And they said their sons were killed in the fighting in Warsaw.
They were officers in the Polish army.
This exchange became very sad.
They bombed our city, and fortunately, all the bombs fell in the fields.
Our town was a small town but a beautiful town.
The streets were cobblestones.
The sidewalks were nice flowers, cement sidewalks.
The-- the houses were mostly single- and two-family houses.
It was a peaceful town.
Next town to us was city of Rovno, with it 50,000 to 60,000 inhabitants, of which 2/3 were Jewish people.
Over there, they had gymnasium.
They had more institutions of learning.
They had a yeshiva.
And they had a lot of teams, you know, soccer teams and boxing teams.

We-- in those days, we also had a Zionist movement, where people on the-- from Ze'ev Jabotinsky's the Betar group-- used to come to our town, encourage the Jewish people to immigrate to Israel and leave.
Well, it seems like [POLISH] was a little too late, and the people were in a dilemma.
Also, they heard the atrocities of the German army.
They kind of stuck.
Didn't know which direction to go.
The Zionists encouraged to leave.
Some young people left, and they went to Israel in some way.

Everything changed the 17th of September, when Hitler made a pact with Stalin, and the Russian army occupied our area, what you call Eastern Poland, which is Western Ukraine, and incorporate that into the Soviet Union.
All the total population looked at the Russian army, the Red Army, as liberators.
Also, some of the Poles and mostly Ukrainians, nationalistic, were not very happy, but they accepted the fact that it was a liberation, a uniting of Western Ukraine and Eastern Ukraine.
The Polish people were in the-- in the middle because Hitler attacked them.
They didn't know which direction to go 'cause this is only 19 years ago where the new Poland is established after so many years of the Czar, Russian empire occupation.
When the Russian army came in, the-- I must say that the Russian soldiers were very well behaved, very compassionate.
Also, they didn't have any material things because our area was like a free enterprise that you could buy anything, the same as in America.
The people under the Soviet regime in Russia didn't have those luxuries.
So the soldiers who had some money or got some money were good clients.
And they came in.
They bought everything they could find.

This didn't last too long because right behind the Russian army came in what you call the propaganda commissars, with some Russian people trying to install a-- a-- a Soviet administration, where they nationalized all of the businesses.
And my folks wound up working at a distribution center.
They'd even occupied most parts of our house for some of the which what you'd call a mayor in the city, until they get established.
Immediately, the education was stopped.
No more Tarbut and no nationalistic school.
We had to accept the Russian curriculum, which was basically a lot of propaganda, but mostly was not a bad curriculum.
The demand was great for literature, math.
And all the subjects were taught quite well, and the teachers were quite good.
And the discipline-- very high discipline.
As youngsters, we accepted that.
We [? started ?] in the school-- in the school, and our life went on.
It's more or less the same-- you know, attending school.
We had some more meetings, you know, because the school demanded meetings to create-- to-- to spread their propaganda.
They ran more dance in the-- in school-- you know, parties at the school.
But the sports activities were basically the same.
And I remember somebody used to try to have the youngsters work in the fields-- you know, get them to help the farmers.
Our vacations, we used to spend, basically, at my grandmother's because the city [POLISH] was surrounded by forest, and it's supposed to be a vacation town because of pure air.
All this changed in June 21-- in June 21, 1941.
The German army occupied our area.
The Ukrainian soldiers, [? capitalists, ?] and joined the Germans.
The Jews and the Poles were being murdered and hunted.
Immediately, the Germans installed the [POLISH] commissar in-- in charge of the city.
They set up a new establishment, a new government, in the-- with the headquarters in Rovno, which was the headquarters for a certain region incorporating Rovno and Dubno and certain towns.
I'm sorry.
And then the people were extremely nervous.
They didn't know what's going to happen next.
They were not in the city for more than two months, where they hunted down 400 people, who I would say, most of them, 380 Jews and 20 Christians.
They took them in a valley behind the cement factory and shot them in cold blood and machine-gunned them.
I was able to escape through a window and ran away from that.
And when they chased me, one of my Polish friends showed the Ukrainian militiamen the wrong direction.
Instead of telling them I went right, they told them I went to the left side.
I went into the wheat fields.
And at night, I came back to the house.
Everything-- the-- the-- the action, what do you call it, was over.
That happened in August, I believe, of 1941.
Nothing happened to the house.
They were hunting people, just going into homes on the streets and picking up people, mostly Jews, you know?
And put them in on the trucks.
They chased.
It-- I saw that, so I ran away.
They were not-- they not opportunity to pick me up.
I-- I jumped through the back window, running to the--
--wheat fields, you know?
And as I found out, when they said, where is he, one of the Polish boys told them, he went to the other direction.
My father escaped also, and my grandfather escape.
But they shot my father's oldest brother in that particular action.
Around the end of August, or the beginning of September, our city saw a German engineer.
That had set up an office and explained to the people that they're going to have two German companies.
One was called Joseph Aung, A-U-N-G, and the other one Todt Organisation.
They going to employ a lot of people.
They're going to work in the railroad station.
They're going to help build bunkers and-- and work in the station to build things for support of the German army.
Actually, that turned out to be like a forced labor camp, you know, where I went to work with my dad and my mother.
And all the Jews from the area worked in those two organizations.
Even some Ukrainian farmers worked there, too.
We were treated differently because we had to wear a star on the front in a-- and on the back, a-- a yellow emblem to identify the Jews.
We couldn't walk on the sidewalks.
We had to walk in the street.
And the labor was very difficult.
At the age of 13 or 14, I was at the time, I had to carry 20 bricks up to two, three stories or given a hammer to break up the frozen ground.
And the-- the-- some of the Jewish girls, they used to make cement and throw them over their heads.
Or they would just act like a horse and buggy.
They would use them as horses and put a buggy with everything.
And they would have to drag it through the street.
The commissar was very difficult.
And the-- the Ukrainian militia was quite bad.
And even, at one point, was a-- somebody working for my dad.
He had a son joined the militia and was cleaning his machine gun-- his gun.
And my father says, Peter, would you kill me, too?
He says, Louis, I like to kill.
I like to kill anybody.
Doesn't matter to me who.
My father wasn't nervous.
But he was in-- a good soldier in the army.
So he kind of did something with his foot.
And the bullet went off, and the guy was killed.
And it turn out to be that it was an accident.
And apparently, the engineer [? grab it ?] and whatever.
They took it all as an accident, and nothing happened to my dad.
Well, the guy was-- it was an accident.
He got-- and he-- and he was dead.
I don't think so.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think so.
I-- I don't know how it happened, what had happened.
It just-- that incident happened.

During the occupation, some of the refugees that ran away from Prague and Vienna couldn't take it anymore, and some committed suicide, especially when one day, a young girl-- she was the daughter of the manager of the cement factory, a beautiful girl about 17 or 18 years old-- your name was Hannah Prusak, P-R-U-S-A-K-- was raped and shot in the middle of the street.
Apparently, she was raped by Ukrainian militiamen.
And-- but then Hungarian-- the Germans officers found out who the militiamen were.
And they beat them up so badly.
But then it was blamed on the Jews.
And they came to Schleifstein, the head of the Judenrat, and asked him to give them 10 hostages.
And they took 10 older Jews, and they killed them.
Another incident I remember, there was a carpenter, a nice carpenter by the name of [POLISH] that the Todt Organisation want him to work for them.
And the Aung one work for them.
To resolve the problem, they took him in the street, and they hung him.
One-- no, this was a Jewish carpenter.
Yes.
That the Todt Organisation want him to work for the Todt Organisation.
The Aung wanted him for the Aung Organisation.
And something was wrong with the commissar, with-- the next day, they took him out.
They hung him in front of everybody as an example to obey, you know, the authorities.
Well, they did not disobey.
The guy was caught in the middle.
One guy says, you work for me.
The other guys says, you work for me.
So when he went to work for the other guy, the commissar hung him.
And I have a picture of that you can see in there.
It describes the details.
That went on--
